quizQuestion: When the electronegativity difference between two bonding atoms exceeds 1.7, what type of chemical bond is formed?
explanation: An electronegativity difference greater than 1.7 indicates that one atom attracts bonding electrons so strongly that electrons are effectively transferred rather than shared, producing an ionic bond. This threshold corresponds to roughly 50% ionic character in the bond.

quizQuestion: A bond between two atoms with an electronegativity difference between 0 and 0.4 is classified as what type of bond?
explanation: When electronegativity differences are very small (0–0.4), electrons are shared nearly equally and no significant dipole forms, producing a nonpolar covalent bond. The H–H bond (difference = 0) and C–H bond (difference ≈ 0.4) are classic examples.

quizQuestion: What model describes metallic bonding as positively charged metal cations surrounded by a mobile 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons?
explanation: In the electron sea model, metal atoms release their valence electrons into a shared pool. This delocalization explains metallic properties: the mobile electrons carry electrical current (conductivity), and the cations can slide past each other without breaking bonds (malleability and ductility).

quizQuestion: When the electronegativity difference between two bonded atoms falls between 0.4 and 1.7, the bond is classified as what type?
explanation: A polar covalent bond forms when electrons are shared unequally but not fully transferred. The more electronegative atom carries a partial negative charge (δ–) and the less electronegative atom carries a partial positive charge (δ+). The O–H bond (difference ≈ 1.4) in water is a familiar example.

quizQuestion: Covalent bonds typically form between what combination of elements?
explanation: Covalent bonds form when both atoms have similar electronegativity — typically when both are nonmetals. Because neither atom has a strong tendency to give up electrons entirely, they share them. Examples include O2, N2, H2O, and CO2, all composed of nonmetallic elements.

quizQuestion: On a potential energy diagram for two bonding atoms, the distance at which potential energy reaches its minimum value is called what?
explanation: As two atoms approach, potential energy decreases due to attractive forces until it reaches a minimum — the equilibrium bond length. At this distance, attractive and repulsive forces between the nuclei and electrons are balanced. Moving the atoms closer increases nuclear repulsion; moving them apart increases energy by stretching the bond.

quizQuestion: Of all bond types between the same two atoms, which has the highest bond energy and the shortest bond length?
explanation: Higher bond order means more electron density shared between nuclei, pulling them closer together (shorter bond length) and requiring more energy to break (higher bond energy). The N≡N triple bond (945 kJ/mol) is dramatically stronger than the N=N double bond (418 kJ/mol) or N–N single bond (163 kJ/mol).

quizQuestion: Breaking a chemical bond is always what type of process with respect to energy?
explanation: Bond breaking always requires an input of energy to overcome the electrostatic attraction holding the atoms together — it is always endothermic. Conversely, bond formation releases energy (exothermic). The net enthalpy change of a reaction reflects the balance between energy absorbed breaking reactant bonds and energy released forming product bonds.

quizQuestion: As the bond order between two atoms increases from single to double to triple, what happens to the bond length?
explanation: Greater bond order means more shared electron pairs pulling the nuclei closer together, reducing internuclear distance. For carbon-carbon bonds: C–C ≈ 154 pm, C=C ≈ 134 pm, C≡C ≈ 120 pm. This inverse relationship between bond order and bond length is a core concept in AP Chemistry Unit 2.

quizQuestion: Ionic compounds form a three-dimensional, repeating arrangement of oppositely charged ions called what?
explanation: In an ionic solid, cations and anions alternate in a regular three-dimensional pattern called a crystal lattice. The geometry of the lattice (cubic, face-centered cubic, etc.) depends on the relative sizes and charges of the ions. This ordered structure maximizes electrostatic attractions and minimizes repulsions.

quizQuestion: As the charges on ions in an ionic compound increase (e.g., going from NaCl to MgO), what happens to the lattice energy?
explanation: Lattice energy is proportional to the product of ion charges. MgO has Mg²⁺ and O²– ions (charges ×2 and ×2), giving it a much higher lattice energy (3795 kJ/mol) than NaCl with its +1 and –1 charges (787 kJ/mol). Higher lattice energy means stronger ionic solid, higher melting point, and lower solubility in most solvents.

quizQuestion: Ionic solids like NaCl shatter when struck because shifting layers brings like charges into alignment, causing repulsion. This makes them what?
explanation: When a mechanical force shifts one layer of an ionic crystal relative to an adjacent layer, ions of the same charge line up and strongly repel each other, causing the crystal to cleave or shatter. This is in stark contrast to metals, where layers of atoms can slide past each other without disrupting the electron sea.

correctAnswer: When dissolved in water or melted
acceptableAlternatives: In aqueous solution or molten state
distractors: Only when heated below melting point
Only when exposed to UV light
quizQuestion: Ionic solids do not conduct electricity in the solid state, but they do conduct electricity under what conditions?
explanation: In the solid state, ions are locked in the lattice and cannot migrate. When the compound is dissolved in water or melted, the ions are freed to move through solution or liquid, allowing electrical current to flow. This is why ionic compounds are called electrolytes — they conduct electricity when dissolved.

quizQuestion: In brass, zinc atoms replace some copper atoms in the crystal lattice because the atoms are similar in size. What type of alloy is brass?
explanation: In a substitutional alloy, atoms of the added element are close in size to the host metal atoms (within ~15%) and replace them at lattice sites. Brass (copper-zinc) and bronze (copper-tin) are classic examples. The substituted atoms disrupt the regularity of the lattice, often increasing hardness compared to the pure metal.

quizQuestion: Steel is formed when small carbon atoms occupy the gaps between iron atoms in the metal lattice. What type of alloy is steel?
explanation: In an interstitial alloy, small atoms (like carbon, nitrogen, or hydrogen) fit into the spaces between the larger host metal atoms rather than replacing them. Steel is the most important example: carbon atoms in the iron lattice impede dislocation movement, dramatically increasing hardness and tensile strength compared to pure iron.

quizQuestion: Because the electron sea allows metal cation layers to slide past each other without breaking the bonding interaction, metals exhibit what physical properties?
explanation: In metallic bonding, the mobile electron sea surrounds cations uniformly. When a force shifts one layer of cations relative to another, the delocalized electrons simply reflow around the new arrangement, maintaining the bond. This allows metals to be hammered into sheets (malleability) or drawn into wires (ductility) without fracturing.

quizQuestion: The octet rule states that main-group atoms in Lewis structures tend to form bonds until they are surrounded by how many electrons?
explanation: The octet rule reflects the stability of having a full valence shell — analogous to the electron configuration of the nearest noble gas. Most second-period nonmetals (C, N, O, F) strictly obey the octet rule. Hydrogen is a notable exception, needing only two electrons (a duet) to complete its 1s shell.

quizQuestion: Which molecule is a classic example of an electron-deficient octet exception, where the central atom has only six electrons around it in the Lewis structure?
explanation: Boron has only three valence electrons and forms three bonds with fluorine in BF3, giving boron just six electrons — an incomplete octet. Boron's small size and inability to accommodate more than four bonds makes it electron-deficient. This makes BF3 a potent Lewis acid, readily accepting an electron pair from Lewis bases.

quizQuestion: Which molecule has an expanded octet with 12 electrons around the central sulfur atom, requiring d-orbital participation to accommodate six bonding pairs?
explanation: Sulfur in SF6 forms six bonds with fluorine atoms, placing 12 electrons around sulfur. This is possible because sulfur is in the third period and has accessible d orbitals that can expand its valence shell beyond eight electrons. SF6 is an extremely stable, dense, and electrically insulating gas used in high-voltage equipment.

correctAnswer: Add one electron per negative charge
acceptableAlternatives: Add electrons equal to the negative charge
Add extra electrons for negative charge
distractors: Remove one electron per negative charge
Ignore the charge when counting electrons
Add one bond per negative charge
Remove one bond per negative charge
Add a lone pair to the central atom only
Distribute charge equally across all atoms
Subtract one electron for each bond formed
quizQuestion: When drawing the Lewis structure of a negatively charged polyatomic ion like NO3–, how do you account for the negative charge in the total electron count?
explanation: Each unit of negative charge on a polyatomic ion represents one extra electron beyond what the neutral atoms contribute. For NO3– (charge = –1), you add 1 electron to the sum of valence electrons from N (5) and the three O atoms (3 × 6 = 18), giving 5 + 18 + 1 = 24 total electrons to distribute in the Lewis structure.

quizQuestion: In a Lewis structure, pairs of valence electrons that belong to a single atom and are not shared between atoms are called what?
explanation: Lone pairs (also called non-bonding pairs) are electron pairs that remain on one atom and do not participate in covalent bonding. They are critical for determining molecular geometry in VSEPR theory because they occupy space and repel bonding pairs, compressing bond angles. Water has two lone pairs on oxygen.

quizQuestion: Which triatomic molecule with a bent shape has two equivalent resonance structures because the double bond can be drawn on either oxygen–oxygen bond?
explanation: Ozone (O3) cannot be represented accurately by a single Lewis structure because both O–O bonds are experimentally identical (bond length ≈ 128 pm), intermediate between a single bond (148 pm) and double bond (121 pm). Two resonance structures are drawn and the actual molecule is a resonance hybrid with delocalized electron density equally distributed over both bonds.

correctAnswer: Valence electrons minus lone pair electrons minus half the bonding electrons
distractors: Valence electrons minus total bonding electrons
Total electrons minus lone pair electrons
Bonding electrons minus lone pair electrons
Valence electrons minus all surrounding electrons
quizQuestion: The formal charge on an atom in a Lewis structure is calculated as what?
explanation: Formal charge = (valence electrons of free atom) – (lone pair electrons on atom) – ½(bonding electrons shared with atom). It assigns bonding electrons equally between partners. The preferred Lewis structure minimizes formal charges and places negative formal charges on more electronegative atoms.

quizQuestion: When a molecule is best described by multiple Lewis structures of equal or similar energy, the actual bonding involves what — electrons that are spread over more than two atoms?
explanation: Resonance structures indicate that electrons in pi bonds are not confined between any one pair of atoms but are delocalized over the entire resonance system. In benzene, for example, the six pi electrons are delocalized over all six carbons, making all C–C bonds equivalent at 140 pm (intermediate between single and double). Delocalization lowers the molecule's energy below any single Lewis structure.

distractors: Maximizes the number of lone pairs
Places all electrons on the central atom
Gives the central atom an octet first
Places positive charge on the most electronegative atom
Maximizes bond order on terminal atoms
quizQuestion: When choosing among multiple valid Lewis structures for the same molecule, the preferred (most accurate) structure is the one that does what with formal charges?
explanation: The preferred Lewis structure minimizes formal charges, ideally giving every atom a formal charge of zero. When nonzero formal charges are unavoidable, negative formal charges should reside on more electronegative atoms. For CO2, the structure with C=O double bonds (all atoms at formal charge 0) is preferred over the structure with a C–O single bond and C≡O triple bond (which creates nonzero formal charges).

quizQuestion: CH4 has four bonding pairs and no lone pairs around carbon. According to VSEPR theory, what is the molecular geometry of methane?
explanation: Four electron domains arranged to minimize repulsion point toward the corners of a tetrahedron. With no lone pairs, the molecular geometry matches the electron geometry exactly — tetrahedral, with H–C–H bond angles of 109.5°. This geometry also describes NH4+ and CCl4.

quizQuestion: NH3 has three bonding pairs and one lone pair on nitrogen. What is the molecular geometry of ammonia?
explanation: Although the four electron domains in NH3 adopt a tetrahedral arrangement, the molecular geometry describes only atom positions — three H atoms form the base of a pyramid with N at the apex. The lone pair compresses the H–N–H bond angles to 107°, slightly less than the tetrahedral ideal of 109.5°, because lone pairs exert stronger repulsion than bonding pairs.

quizQuestion: A carbon atom in ethylene (C2H4) has three electron domains — two C–H bonds and one C=C bond. What hybridization does this carbon adopt?
explanation: Three electron domains require three hybrid orbitals, formed by mixing one s and two p orbitals — called sp2 hybridization. The three sp2 orbitals point toward the corners of a triangle at 120° (trigonal planar geometry). The unhybridized p orbital perpendicular to this plane overlaps with the adjacent carbon's p orbital to form the pi bond of the double bond.

correctAnswer: One sigma bond and one pi bond
acceptableAlternatives: A sigma bond and a pi bond
distractors: One sigma bond and two pi bonds
Two sigma bonds and one pi bond
quizQuestion: A double bond between two atoms consists of how many sigma bonds and how many pi bonds?
explanation: Every covalent bond, regardless of order, contains exactly one sigma (σ) bond formed by direct, end-on orbital overlap along the bond axis. Double bonds add one pi (π) bond formed by side-on (lateral) overlap of parallel p orbitals. Triple bonds consist of one σ and two π bonds. Pi bonds are weaker than sigma bonds because lateral overlap is less effective than axial overlap.

quizQuestion: SF6 has six bonding pairs and no lone pairs around the central sulfur atom. What molecular geometry does SF6 adopt?
explanation: Six electron domains minimize repulsion by pointing toward the faces of an octahedron — four in a square plane plus one above and one below. With no lone pairs, molecular geometry matches electron geometry: octahedral, with all F–S–F bond angles of 90°. SF6 requires sp3d2 hybridization of the sulfur atom.
